key_insight: A two-stage finite element scheme decouples curvature-driven motion from mesh update so the constrained Helfrich flow preserves energy, volume, and surface area.
engineering_relevance: This gives a practical simulation tool for evolving biological or membrane-like interfaces where geometric conservation and stable long-time behavior are essential.
method: Compute normal velocity from a curvature evolution equation with soft volume/area constraints.
Update the mesh using the computed normal velocity plus a BGN-type tangential velocity for mesh quality.
Apply a time-weighted interface normal and area-correction multiplier to enforce exact volume and area conservation; solve the resulting nonlinear step with Newton iteration.
evidence_signal: The paper reports numerical experiments on 2D curves and 3D surfaces, including nonsmooth initial data and nonzero spontaneous curvature, to verify accuracy and structure preservation.
watch_item: The correction stage is nonlinear and requires Newton solves, so robustness and cost may depend on timestep choice and initialization.
paper_title: Geometric structure-preserving parametric finite element approximations for the constrained Helfrich flow
